critique.summary: The article investigates the federal government's approval of Microsoft's Government Community Cloud High (GCC High) product despite significant cybersecurity concerns. It reveals that FedRAMP authorized the product even though internal reviewers described it as insecure and incomplete, highlighting systemic issues in the federal cloud security authorization process and potential conflicts of interest.
mainPoints: FedRAMP authorized Microsoft's GCC High cloud product despite internal government cybersecurity experts expressing a lack of confidence in its security.
Microsoft failed to provide detailed security documentation, including data flow diagrams critical for assessing encryption and data protection.
The authorization process was prolonged over several years, with FedRAMP reviewers repeatedly receiving incomplete information from Microsoft.
Third-party assessment organizations hired by Microsoft expressed concerns about the inability to fully vet GCC High but were constrained by conflicts of interest.
The Justice Department authorized GCC High and supported Microsoft in pushing FedRAMP to approve the product despite ongoing security concerns.
FedRAMP's staff and budget cuts have weakened its ability to rigorously assess cloud products, turning it into more of a rubber stamp for industry.
Microsoft used China-based engineers to maintain sensitive government cloud systems, violating Pentagon rules, which was revealed by investigative reporting rather than internal oversight.
The article highlights broader issues in federal cybersecurity oversight, including potential conflicts of interest and insufficient enforcement mechanisms.
critique.truthfulness: The article is based on extensive investigative reporting by ProPublica, citing internal government documents, emails, meeting minutes, and interviews with multiple former and current government officials and contractors. The claims about FedRAMP's authorization of GCC High despite security concerns, Microsoft's incomplete documentation, and the involvement of third-party assessors are corroborated by multiple sources and align with known challenges in federal cloud security oversight. The article also references related public incidents, such as the SolarWinds hack and the Justice Department's cyber-fraud initiative, which are factual. Microsoft's responses and denials are included, providing balance. Overall, the article's factual claims are well-supported, though some details rely on anonymous sources typical in investigative journalism.
considerations: The article does not deeply explore Microsoft's technical responses or improvements made post-authorization beyond general statements.
It does not provide detailed perspectives from FedRAMP officials currently managing the program beyond official statements.
The broader context of federal cybersecurity challenges and resource constraints affecting other cloud providers is only briefly mentioned.
Potential legal or contractual implications for Microsoft or other contractors from these findings are not fully addressed.
The article focuses on GCC High and Microsoft but does not extensively compare to other cloud providers' authorization processes.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the controversial approval of Microsoft's cloud service by federal cyber experts despite significant concerns about its security and usability. Commenters highlight issues such as bureaucratic inertia, conflicts of interest, vendor lock-in, and the complexity and reliability problems of Azure compared to other cloud providers.

comments.author: jbombadil
comments.text:
> [...]And because federal agencies were allowed to deploy the product during the review, GCC High spread across the government as well as the defense industry. By late 2024, FedRAMP reviewers concluded that they had little choice but to authorize the technology — not because their questions had been answered or their review was complete, but largely on the grounds that Microsoft’s product was already being used across Washington.

This sounds like the crux of the issue. The combination of: "tool can be used during analysis" and "analysis takes long" shifts the barrier of rejection from "is this tool safe?" to "is this tool so unsafe that we're willing to start a fight with a lot of other government agencies to remove it, find an alternative, etc?".

Not criticizing FedRAMP. Proper security review takes time. And probably more when dealing with vendors.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully explains how procedural realities can pressure approval decisions, aligning well with bureaucratic dynamics described in the article.

comments.author: dogleash
comments.text:
> By late 2024, FedRAMP reviewers concluded that they had little choice but to authorize the technology — not because their questions had been answered or their review was complete, but largely on the grounds that Microsoft’s product was already being used across Washington.

The article talks a lot about conflicts of interest, but this is the line I went looking for. A bureaucracy fighting itself over goal prioritization, and what's a necessary roadblock vs red tape is the less sexy but more meaningful problem at the core of this.

Once the government decided they wanted the product, they were going to find a patsy.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately identifies systemic bureaucratic pressures and conflicts of interest influencing the approval process.

comments.author: sysops9x
comments.text: The vendor lock-in angle is the real story. Once you have AD, Exchange, Teams, and Azure all tangled together, the exit cost is enormous. I've watched orgs get approval for a full cloud migration based on cost savings projections that completely ignored the engineering cost of the decade-long migration that follows. The security issues are real but the procurement inertia is what keeps them renewing.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly highlights the economic and organizational factors that perpetuate reliance on Microsoft cloud services despite known problems.

comments.author: iscoelho
comments.text:
Microsoft has never been good at security, and that is why their centralization to cloud is absolutely terrifying.

I'm reminded of Storm-0558 [1] where a stolen signing key was able to forge authentication tokens for any MSA / Azure AD / Government AD user. They downplayed the severity. Just imagine if that level of access was used to pull a Stryker on a nation-wide scale. That is an economic disaster waiting to happen.

[1] https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2023/07/14/ana...
comments.analysis: The reference to a known security incident supports the claim about Microsoft's security shortcomings, though the tone is somewhat alarmist.

comments.author: debarshri
comments.text:
Recently tried using Entra ID. There are 12 ways to enforce MFA, 20 days ways to disable users, 4 ways to authenticate users, Add conditional access stuff with 50 variables and templates etc.

You can customize the way you want. After configuring it, my colleagues could not log in. Thats one way to secure your organization.
comments.analysis: This practical experience underscores the complexity of Microsoft's security tools, which can hinder usability and adoption.

comments.author: kevincloudsec
comments.text: the product got deployed across the government while the security review was still in progress. then fedramp approved it because it was already everywhere. seem like i saw a lobbyist or two with a broom sweeping something under a rug...
comments.analysis: This comment aligns with the article's main point about approval being influenced by existing deployment rather than completed review.

comments.author: markstos
comments.text: Frustrating that FedRAMP is both a pain to get compliant with and also apparently is not a strong signal of actual security.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately reflects common criticisms of FedRAMP's effectiveness and bureaucratic challenges.

comments.author: kqgnkqgn
comments.text:
This is my opinion only, I'm sure some have had different experiences - but:

Azure's success as a cloud provider is mostly a result of their sales team and having an existing relationship with non-technical leadership. "We already pay them for Office and Exchange, let's just buy this new 'cloud' thing from them too".

Azure is barely considered an option at all within tech companies, yet is surprisingly widely adopted by non-technical companies that don't know any better (ie, that don't have a technical / engineering voice or representation within leadership).

AWS = Likely technically the best, for now. Mostly unreasonable pricing, and less motivation to seriously negotiate given they are the 'default' cloud provider for most of the industry. Kind of feels like they have peaked though, and are slipping more recently. Inevitable, or bad leadership changes?

OCI = New-comer, attractive pricing and hungry for business. Might be able to avoid mistakes other providers have made? Reliability struggles though. Parent company has a bad reputation in some circles - but probably not with decision makers. Making huge (unwise?) investments - that will either come crashing down in 5 years, or seriously pay off. Layoffs, but going for massive growth...huh?

GCP = Notably different underlying technical choices than other providers. Folks are maybe a bit less pragmatic, and more academic. This helps them in unique services (Spanner?) but hurts in most other areas. They've matured, and are btwn AWS and OCI in reliability. They are probably not as hungry for business as they should be given how far behind they are.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a balanced industry perspective on cloud provider market dynamics, consistent with broader consensus.

comments.author: kajecounterhack
comments.text:
+10000 that Azure is a steaming pile of shit. Like what's this -- `azcopy` broken at head, and the working one doesn't guarantee correctness after a copy (99.6% copied successfully! good luck figuring out what went wrong!) compare that to migrating data with GCS or S3 -- they provide first class tools that do it right quickly (aws-cli, gsutil).

Want a VM? You'll also need this network security group, network interface, network manager, ip, virtual network... and maybe it'll be connected to the internet so you can SSH in? Compare to GCP or EC2 -- you just pick an instance and start it. You can SSH in directly, or even do it in the browser.

Billing also a nightmare: if you're running a startup, AWS and Google make it relatively easy to see how many credits you have left. The Azure dashboard makes you navigate a maze, and the button to click that says "Azure Credits" is _invisible_ for 30s until ostensibly some backend system finds your credits, then it magically shows up. Most people don't wait around and just assume there's no button.

And if you click it, maybe you will happen to be in the correct billing profile, maybe not! Don't get confused: billing profile and billing scope are different concepts too! And in your invoice, costs just magically get deducted, until they don't. No mention of any credits. Credits inaccessible through API (claude tried everything).

VMs, bucket storage, and copying data are the _simplest_ parts of the stack. Why would anyone bother trying to use other services if they can't get these right?

They literally give startups 2x the credits as GCP, 20x the credits of AWS and nobody wants to use them.
comments.analysis: While strongly worded, the comment provides detailed user experience issues that are corroborated by other reports on Azure's usability problems.

comments.author: iamleppert
comments.text:
Azure is easily the most expensive, least reliable and worst cloud available. It's borderline scam. An example today, I provisioned high IOPS SSDs (supposedly) and what is actually connected to the instance? A spinning hard drive! I didn't even know they were still made, but I guess Azure uses them and scams their users into thinking you're getting an SSD for $700/mo when its really an old hard drive.

I would warn anyone far and wide to avoid Azure at all costs, especially if you are a startup. And especially if you are doing any kind of AI because the only GPUs they have available are ancient and also crazy over-priced.

If I cared more, I'd try to migrate away from Azure. But I don't, and that's probably Azure's business model at this point.
comments.analysis: This comment reflects a frustrated user perspective with specific examples, though some claims may be anecdotal and require further verification.
